sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.content: Peritoneal mesothelioma (PeM) is a rare cancer originating in mesothelial surfaces of the peritoneum, accounting for approximately 15% of all mesotheliomas, with the remaining approximately 85% arising from the pleura [1]. It is estimated that PeM occurs in approximately 300 to 400 people in the United States every year [10]. The mean age at diagnosis is approximately 69 years [11]. Unlike pleural mesothelioma, which is more common in males, PeM is diagnosed in approximately equal numbers of males and females [11]. PeM may occur in younger patients compared with pleural mesothelioma, which typically occurs in older patients [11]. Patients with PeM commonly present with abdominal signs and symptoms, including ascites (occurring in 77% of patients), abdominal pain (69%), abdominal distension, and an abdominal mass (30%) [11,21,38]. Patients often have a high symptom burden compared with patients who have other types of cancer [11]. Additional presenting symptoms include weight loss, fatigue, anorexia, asthenia, nausea, early satiety, and intestinal obstruction [11,21,38]. Although PeM can spread extensively within the abdomen, it less commonly metastasizes beyond the abdominal cavity [24]. One-year overall survival is approximately 46% in patients with PeM, and 5-year overall survival is about 20%; cure is rare [2,11,121-124]. Survival is improved for patients who are able to undergo complete cytoreductive surgery (CRS) with intraperitoneal chemotherapy [12-16]. Many patients with PeM have idiopathic disease [20]. Similar to pleural mesothelioma, the histologic subtypes of PeM include epithelioid (most common), sarcomatoid, and biphasic (also known as mixed, containing both epithelioid and sarcomatoid components) [4,17-19]. Patients with epithelioid histology have better outcomes than those with either biphasic or sarcomatoid histologies [15].

symptoms.name: Ascites
symptoms.detail: Accumulation of fluid within the peritoneal cavity, present in approximately 77% of patients at diagnosis. May cause abdominal distension, early satiety, and discomfort.

symptoms.name: Abdominal pain
symptoms.detail: Present in approximately 69% of patients. May be diffuse or localized, and may be related to peritoneal tumor burden, adhesions, or organ involvement.

symptoms.name: Abdominal distension
symptoms.detail: Visible or palpable enlargement of the abdomen, which may result from ascites, tumor mass effect, or both.

symptoms.name: Abdominal mass
symptoms.detail: Palpable mass noted on physical examination, present in approximately 30% of patients.

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unintentional weight loss, which may be associated with anorexia, cachexia, or high symptom burden.

symptoms.name: Fatigue
symptoms.detail: Persistent tiredness and lack of energy, common in mesothelioma and often associated with high symptom burden.

symptoms.name: Anorexia
symptoms.detail: Loss of appetite, which may contribute to weight loss and nutritional compromise.

symptoms.name: Asthenia
symptoms.detail: Physical weakness and lack of strength.

symptoms.name: Nausea
symptoms.detail: Feeling of sickness that may lead to vomiting; may result from peritoneal irritation, bowel involvement, or ascites.

symptoms.name: Early satiety
symptoms.detail: Feeling of fullness after eating small amounts of food, often related to ascites or peritoneal disease compressing the stomach.

symptoms.name: Intestinal obstruction
symptoms.detail: Blockage of the bowel that may result from peritoneal tumor encasing or compressing bowel loops. May present with nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and inability to pass flatus or stool.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Accumulation of fluid within the peritoneal cavity, present in approximately 77% of patients at diagnosis. May cause abdominal distension, early satiety, and discomfort.
